items.q: What causes global sea level rise?
items.correct: Two main factors: thermal expansion of warming seawater, and the melting of land-based ice (glaciers and ice sheets) adding water to oceans.
items.misconception: Sea level rises mainly because Arctic sea ice is melting.
items.random_wrong: Sea level rises because of underwater volcanoes pushing it up.
items.why: Floating sea ice already displaces its weight in water — its melting does not raise sea level. The contributors are LAND ice (Greenland, Antarctica) and thermal expansion.

items.q: Why is the Brandt Line considered outdated?
items.correct: Drawn in 1980, it predates the rise of NICs (China, India, Brazil) and globalisation — countries like South Korea, Singapore and Chile no longer fit neatly on the 'poor south' side.
items.misconception: The Brandt Line still accurately divides rich and poor countries today.
items.random_wrong: The Brandt Line is the boundary between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
items.why: The world has changed: BRICs have grown, some HICs have stagnated. A simple north-south divide cannot capture this. Many geographers prefer the term 'Global North/Global South' loosely or reject the binary altogether.

items.q: What happens during an El Nino event?
items.correct: Trade winds weaken, warm water that normally pools in the western Pacific spreads east, disrupting weather patterns globally — drought in Australia, floods in South America.
items.misconception: El Nino is when the ocean gets colder near Peru.
items.random_wrong: El Nino is the name of a specific hurricane that hit Mexico.
items.why: El Nino brings WARM water to Peru — the cold-water state is La Nina. Students mix up the two phases; the name 'the Christ child' refers to warm currents at Christmas.
The answer examiners want is: Maritime boundaries and rights. “Air traffic rights” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which year did the Permanent Court of Arbitration rule against China's nine-dash line?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2016. “2010” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Approximately what percentage of the South China Sea does China claim via the nine-dash line?
items.why: Exact answer: 90. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
